summary: Postinstall credential harvester exfiltrates secrets, SSH keys, and wallet material to Telegram bot
details: @wagni_bot/opensea-sdk@1.0.0 impersonates the legitimate OpenSea JavaScript SDK (opensea-js). The package's runtime module is empty (index.js is only `module.exports = {}`), and the entire behavior is contained in a postinstall lifecycle script (postinstall.js) that fires automatically on `npm install`. On install, the script recursively walks `process.cwd()`, the user's home directory, and `~/.ssh`, matching files by the keywords `.env`, `.cred`, `.pem`, `.key`, `mnemonic`, `seed`, `wallet`, `secret`, `id_rsa`, `id_ed25519`, reads their contents, and POSTs the file contents together with `os.hostname()`, `os.userInfo().username`, platform/release, `cwd`, `__dirname`, and filtered environment variables to a hardcoded Telegram Bot API endpoint (`https://api.telegram.org/bot<token>/sendMessage`, bot id 8804087989, chat id 892359416). A separate 'PACKAGE INSTALLED' beacon transmits host reconnaissance to the same endpoint. This is a credential-theft supply-chain attack combined with a typosquat lure of opensea-js.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
